content.problemStatement:
You are given three positive integers X,Y,X, Y, X,Y, and ZZZ.

In one operation, you can choose any one of these values, and change it to any integer of your choice.

Find the **minimum** number of operations required to make the sequence (X,Y,Z)(X, Y, Z)(X,Y,Z) an arithmetic progression.

Note that (X,Y,Z)(X, Y, Z)(X,Y,Z) is an arithmetic progression if and only if Y‚ąíX=Z‚ąíYY-X = Z-YY‚ąíX=Z‚ąíY.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of testcases.
The first and only line of each testcase contains three space-separated integers X,YX, YX,Y and ZZZ, denoting the three numbers.


Output Format
For each test case, print one line containing a single integer: the **minimum** number of operations such that (X,Y,Z)(X, Y, Z)(X,Y,Z) forms an arithmetic progression.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Y‚ąíX=3Y - X = 3Y‚ąíX=3 and Z‚ąíY=3Z - Y = 3Z‚ąíY=3 which are already equal. So, (X,Y,Z)(X, Y, Z)(X,Y,Z) is already an arithmetic progression, no moves are required.
Test case 222: We use one operation and change ZZZ to 111, so that (X,Y,Z)=(7,4,1)(X, Y, Z) = (7, 4, 1)(X,Y,Z)=(7,4,1).
Now, Y‚ąíX=‚ąí3Y - X = -3Y‚ąíX=‚ąí3 and Z‚ąíY=‚ąí3Z - Y = -3Z‚ąíY=‚ąí3 which are equal, so this is an arithmetic progression.
